items.correct: Attributing human emotions or qualities to nature or inanimate objects to reflect a character's feelings or scene's mood (e.g. angry storms during conflict).
items.misconception: Pathetic fallacy is when an argument in a story is weak or sad.
items.random_wrong: Pathetic fallacy is when characters cry over silly things.
items.why: The term uses 'pathetic' in its old sense — pertaining to emotion (pathos) — not 'feeble'. Students misread it as a value judgement when it is a technical device coined by Ruskin.

items.correct: When the audience knows something a character does not, creating tension between the character's words/actions and the audience's understanding.
items.misconception: Dramatic irony is when something ironic happens in a play.
items.random_wrong: Dramatic irony is when characters speak in dramatic voices.
items.why: Students treat 'dramatic irony' as just irony in a drama. The defining feature is the gap between audience and character knowledge — without that gap, it isn't dramatic irony.

items.correct: When a speaker says one thing but means another — typically the opposite — relying on shared context for the listener to detect the gap.
items.misconception: Verbal irony is just saying something unexpected.
items.random_wrong: Verbal irony is when you make a joke about something.
items.why: Students conflate irony with surprise or humour. Irony specifically involves a gap between literal and intended meaning — a more precise device than 'unexpected'.
Correct answer: Kristeva. “Barthes” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Whose 1973 study described the anxiety of influence?
items.why: Correct answer: Bloom. “Frye” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did Rhys publish Wide Sargasso Sea?
items.why: The real figure is 1966 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
